nativeRange.habitat: Coral and sponge-covered seaward reef slopes; tends to inhabit deeper reef areas and is seldom observed by divers

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on detritus, small invertebrates, microalgae, and sponges in the wild. In captivity tends to accept spirulina, marine algae, nori, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp. Feeding small amounts 3 or more times daily is generally advised. New arrivals can be particularly reluctant to venture from hiding to accept food, and patience is often required during the acclimation period.

summary: The Golden Angelfish (Centropyge aurantia) is a rare, expert-level dwarf angelfish from the Western Pacific, prized for its rich velvet-orange coloration with broken yellow bars. Generally advised for experienced marine aquarists only.
careNotes: The Golden Angelfish is considered one of the more challenging dwarf angelfishes to keep. It tends to inhabit deeper reef slopes in the wild and has a reputation for being particularly cryptic — specimens are seldom seen by divers and are rarely available in the aquarium trade, which contributes to their high market price. New arrivals are commonly too shy to venture out of their hiding places to accept food; dim lighting during initial acclimation, with gradual increases in intensity, is generally advised. Ample live rock arranged into caves and overhangs appears important for long-term health, both for grazing microalgae and for the fish's psychological security. Pristine water quality is reported to be important; sources recommend regular partial water changes. Sources disagree on care level: two rate it 'difficult' and two rate it 'expert-only'; the 'expert' designation is used here given the combination of acclimation difficulty, rarity, and specialized feeding requirements. Sources also disagree on temperament: LiveAquaria and Tropical Fish Keeping describe it as peaceful toward most fish, while Blue Zoo rates it semi-aggressive; 'semi-aggressive' is used here because the species is well-documented to be highly aggressive toward other angelfish and will not generally tolerate conspecifics except as an established mated pair. Reef safety: all sources concur it is reef-safe 'with caution' — it has been known to nip at SPS, LPS, Xenia corals, and clam mantles, especially if underfed. Temperature sources showed a moderate spread: aquarium hobby sources suggest 22.8–26.5°C; FishBase wild habitat data indicates 28–29°C, which likely reflects the warmer waters near its natural depth range. The aquarium ranges are used here as they reflect long-term captive husbandry experience. Tank size: most retail sources list 55 gallons (208 L) as the minimum, but given the species' territorial nature and need for extensive rockwork territory, 250 L is used here as a more appropriate practical minimum. Pairs of this species have been reported to do better in captivity than solitary individuals; some sources suggest keeping as a mated pair if possible. Juveniles show a yellow coloration with electric-blue dorsal and tail outlines that deepens into the characteristic golden-orange hue in adults.
breedingNotes: Centropyge aurantia is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, as are most angelfishes — females can change sex to become dominant males. Captive breeding has been reported as very difficult; males and females cannot generally be distinguished by coloration alone. Broadcast spawning behavior has been observed. Pairs are reported to do notably better in captivity than singletons, though confirmed captive spawning events appear to be uncommon.

additional.name: Blue Zoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, tank size, temperament, diet, and reef safety. Rates care level as expert-only and temperament as semi-aggressive (vs. peaceful at LiveAquaria); semi-aggressive used in final entry as it reflects documented conspecific behavior.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (73–81°F / 22.8–27.2°C), pH, salinity, adult size (3.9 in / 9.9 cm), lifespan (6–10 years), native range, and reef safety. Temperature max averaged with other sources.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family (Pomacanthidae), order (Acanthuriformes), max adult size (10.0 cm TL), native distribution (Western Pacific: northern Great Barrier Reef to Samoa including Indonesia), and wild temperature (28.1–29°C). Wild temperature noted but aquarium-hobby ranges used for waterParameters as they reflect captive husbandry experience.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (4 in / 10 cm), temperature (74–78°F / 23.3–25.6°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), care level (expert), lifespan (often 10+ years), and omnivore diet. Reef safety listed as 'no' here vs. 'with caution' elsewhere — 'with caution' used as the consensus across 3 other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; broadcast spawner. Captive breeding is rarely achieved. Pairs are reported to acclimate and thrive better than solitary individuals.
